title: Add multi-primitive combo challenge levels (Deadlock Empire 'boss fight' style)
description:
GAP from Deadlock Empire comparison: DE's best levels (Dragonfire, Triple Danger, Boss Fight) combine multiple primitives in one challenge — e.g., mutex + semaphore + condition variable in one scenario. Our practice tasks (CON-161-163) each test ONE primitive. Add 3 'boss fight' levels that require understanding multiple primitives simultaneously:
1. Level 1: Mutex + Condition Variable (bounded buffer with CV, not just wait/signal abstraction)
2. Level 2: Semaphore + Barrier (N workers must all acquire permits AND reach barrier before proceeding)
3. Level 3: Lock ordering + CAS + Thread Pool (realistic production scenario combining 3 concepts)
Source: Deadlock Empire boss fight levels (GPL-2).
acceptanceCriteria: 3 multi-primitive challenge levels available in practice mode
Each level requires knowledge of 2+ primitives to solve
Hint system references which primitive concepts to review
Completion unlocks 'Boss Fight' achievement

title: Add concurrent data structure internals demo (counter, linked list, hash table)
description:
GAP from OSTEP Ch28 + JCA: No demo shows HOW concurrent data structures work internally. Our Lock-Free DS task (CON-104) covers lock-free stack/queue, but OSTEP Ch28 teaches LOCKED data structures first: (1) concurrent counter with per-CPU partitioning, (2) concurrent linked list with hand-over-hand locking, (3) concurrent hash table with per-bucket locks. These are interview favorites at Databricks and Google.
Source: OSTEP Ch28 'Lock-based Concurrent Data Structures', JCA ConcurrentHashMap animation.
acceptanceCriteria: New 'Concurrent Data Structures' demo with at least 2 sub-demos
Concurrent counter shows naive (global lock) vs partitioned (per-CPU) approach
Concurrent linked list shows hand-over-hand locking step-by-step
Properties panel explains when to use lock-based vs lock-free DS

title: Add Amdahl's Law interactive calculator and visualization
description:
GAP from JCiP Ch11: Amdahl's Law is THE fundamental law of parallel computing — it determines the maximum speedup possible for any parallelizable workload. No demo covers it. Add an interactive calculator: user sets % parallelizable (slider 0-100%) and thread count (slider 1-64), sees the speedup curve.
Formula: Speedup = 1 / ((1 - P) + P/N) where P = parallel fraction, N = processors.
The visualization should show diminishing returns: '90% parallelizable with 10 threads = 5.3x speedup, but 100 threads only gives 9.2x — that last 10% sequential code is the bottleneck.'
Source: JCiP Ch11, Amdahl 1967.
acceptanceCriteria: Interactive Amdahl's Law calculator with sliders for parallelizable fraction and thread count
Live speedup curve chart updates as sliders change
Shows diminishing returns visually — curve flattens
Properties panel explains practical implications for lock contention

title: Add low-level lock implementation demos: ticket lock, test-and-set, fetch-and-add
description:
GAP from OSTEP Ch27: Our Lock Comparison (SpinLock/Mutex/TTAS) skips the underlying hardware primitives. OSTEP Ch27 teaches: (1) test-and-set (TAS) — the simplest atomic instruction, (2) compare-and-swap (CAS) — already in CON-095, (3) fetch-and-add — atomic increment, (4) ticket lock — fairness via turn-taking using fetch-and-add. Add these as sub-demos under Lock Comparison.
Source: OSTEP Ch27 'Locks', Herlihy Ch7.
acceptanceCriteria: Test-and-set sub-demo showing the atomic read-modify-write in one step
Fetch-and-add sub-demo showing atomic increment
Ticket lock sub-demo showing FIFO fairness via ticket+turn numbers
Properties panel compares fairness: TAS (unfair) vs ticket lock (fair) vs TTAS (reduced contention)

title: Add volatile / safe publication / thread-confinement demo
description:
GAP from JCiP Ch3: Our Memory Model demo (CON-094) covers happens-before and memory barriers but NOT the three fundamental strategies for sharing objects safely: (1) volatile — guarantees visibility but not atomicity, (2) safe publication — using synchronization, final fields, or volatile to make object state visible to other threads, (3) thread confinement — keeping data on one thread (stack confinement, ThreadLocal). These are senior Java interview fundamentals.
Source: JCiP Ch3 'Sharing Objects'.
acceptanceCriteria: Volatile demo shows visibility guarantee: Thread A sets flag=true, Thread B reads flag
Safe publication demo shows unsafe vs safe object construction
Thread confinement demo shows ThreadLocal pattern
Properties panel explains when each strategy applies

title: Add thread pool sizing interactive calculator (Little's Law + practical heuristics)
description:
GAP from JCiP Ch8: No demo helps users SIZE their thread pools. Add an interactive calculator:
- Input: avg task duration, target throughput, CPU count
- Output: recommended pool size using formulas:
  - CPU-bound: N_threads = N_cpus + 1
  - I/O-bound: N_threads = N_cpus * (1 + wait_time/compute_time)
  - Little's Law: N = throughput * latency
- Visualization: show utilization curve as pool size increases
Source: JCiP Ch8 'Sizing Thread Pools', Little's Law.
acceptanceCriteria: Interactive calculator with sliders for task duration, throughput, CPU count
Shows CPU-bound vs I/O-bound recommendations
Little's Law visualization with utilization curve
Properties panel explains over-provisioning risks (context switch overhead)

title: Add goroutine/resource leak visualization as a dedicated demo
description:
GAP from GoTrace: Our goroutine select demo MENTIONS the leak at step 6 ('sender-1 is leaked') but doesn't make it a TEACHING FOCUS. GoTrace has dedicated leak visualization. Add a 'Resource Leaks' demo showing: (1) goroutine leak: spawned goroutine blocked on channel with no reader, (2) timer leak: time.After in loop creating unreferenced timers, (3) connection leak: opened but never closed. Each with visual 'decay' animation showing the resource wasting away.
Source: GoTrace leak visualization, Go blog 'Concurrency Patterns: Context'.
acceptanceCriteria: New 'Resource Leaks' demo with at least 2 leak scenarios
Goroutine leak shows blocked goroutine with visual decay/dimming over time
Properties panel explains detection strategies (runtime.NumGoroutine, pprof)
Cross-reference to Go Goroutines select demo that already shows the leak

title: Add colorblind-safe palette option for all concurrency state colors
description:
EDGE CASE GAP: All concurrency visualizations use red/green color pairs for state distinction (running=#22c55e vs blocked=#ef4444, hold-arrow=#22c55e vs wait-arrow=#ef4444, safe=green vs unsafe=red). These are indistinguishable for deuteranopia (8% of males) and protanopia.
Use IBM's colorblind-safe palette (yellow #FFB000, blue #648FFF, orange #FE6100, purple #785EF0) or add pattern/shape backup. The platform batch-quality.json has a generic 'Colorblind-safe palette option' task but nothing concurrency-specific.
Replace: green→blue, red→orange for ALL state maps when colorblind mode is active.
acceptanceCriteria: Colorblind toggle in settings or auto-detect from OS accessibility
All state colors use IBM colorblind-safe palette when active
Red/green pairs replaced with blue/orange or yellow/purple
Shape/pattern backup on ALL 8 color-only state indicators (from CON-065)

title: Implement light mode support for all concurrency visualizations
description:
EDGE CASE GAP: CON-182 creates CSS custom properties foundation but NEVER implements light mode. All 95 hex colors are designed for dark backgrounds (#1e293b, #334155). In light mode, these will be invisible or low-contrast. Need a complete light mode color set:
- Dark bg fills → light bg fills
- White text → dark text
- Neon accent colors → muted accent colors
- SVG stroke colors adjusted for light backgrounds
Dark mode remains default; light mode activates with platform theme toggle.
acceptanceCriteria: Light mode CSS variables defined for all concurrency state colors
All SVG visualizations readable in light mode (text, fills, strokes)
Toggle works via platform theme setting (ui-store theme preference)
No visual regression in dark mode

title: Add print stylesheet for concurrency module — professors need printable diagrams
description:
EDGE CASE GAP: Zero @media print rules exist in the entire platform. Professors want to print concurrency diagrams for handouts. Currently printing produces: dark backgrounds wasting ink, invisible text, broken interactive controls, and SVG elements that may not render.
Add @media print rules:
- White background, dark text
- Hide playback controls, sidebar, activity bar
- Show only: current visualization state + step description + concept name
- SVG elements use high-contrast print colors
- Each demo prints on one page (page-break-inside: avoid)
acceptanceCriteria: @media print stylesheet shows clean white-background visualization
Playback controls, sidebar, and activity bar hidden in print
SVG visualizations render with print-safe colors (black/gray, no neon)
Each demo fits on one printed page

title: Add responsive breakpoints to ALL 11 remaining demos (not just Event Loop)
description:
EDGE CASE GAP: CON-185 adds responsive breakpoints to Event Loop's grid-cols-4 layout only. The other 11 demos have NO responsive handling. On iPad split-screen (~507px), Dining Philosophers SVG is tiny, Lock Comparison 3-column layout is cramped, and side-by-side demos (DiningPhilosophers naive+ordered, DeadlockDemo deadlock+prevention) stack poorly.
Add responsive breakpoints to all demos:
- <640px: side-by-side layouts stack vertically, SVGs scale to full width
- 640-1024px: side-by-side maintained with smaller gap
- >1024px: current layout preserved
acceptanceCriteria: All side-by-side demos (Philosophers, Deadlock) stack vertically below 640px
Lock Comparison 3-column layout adapts to 1-column below 640px
SVG viewBoxes scale to full container width on narrow screens
No horizontal scrolling at any viewport width down to 320px
